clean_story:
Tiny electron rivers.  Precision tools for cutting, imaging, welding. "Electron beam" describes a stream of high-speed electrons—like water from a hose, but with particles instead of droplets. When focused, these beams act as invisible scalpels, torches, or cameras. If electron_beams can rightly be perceived as industrial lasers’ sharper cousin—
they carve metal with 0. 1mm precision, weld airplane parts without heat warping, and scan surfaces atom by atom in electron microscopes. How?A cathode "gun" fires electrons, magnetic lenses steer them, and the target—metal, silicon, or tissue—reacts on impact. Example:Your phone’s microchip was likely etched by an electron beam, shrinking circuits to nanoscale. NASA uses them to 3D-print rocket nozzles from titanium powder. Three related subjects are plasma physics, nanofabrication, and X-ray tubes.
